title: Introduction to GPU Computing
description: Understand why parallel computing matters, how GPU architecture differs from CPUs, and when to use heterogeneous computing. Covers Moore's Law, the power wall, SIMT execution, and Amdahl's Law.

sections.title: Why Parallel Computing
sections.content:
## The End of the Free Lunch

For decades, software developers enjoyed a remarkable free ride. Every 18 months or so, CPUs got faster -- your same code ran quicker without changing a line. This phenomenon was driven by **Moore's Law**, the observation by Intel co-founder Gordon Moore in 1965 that the number of transistors on a chip doubles roughly every two years.

But here is the crucial distinction most people miss: **Moore's Law is about transistor count, not clock speed.** For a long time, more transistors meant higher clock frequencies. CPUs went from 5 MHz (Intel 8086, 1978) to 200 MHz (Pentium Pro, 1995) to 3.8 GHz (Pentium 4, 2004). Then something hit a wall.

### The Power Wall

The relationship between clock frequency and power consumption is roughly:

```
Power ~ Voltage^2 * Frequency
```

As frequency increases, voltage must also increase to maintain signal integrity. The result is that **power consumption grows cubically** with clock speed. By 2004, the Pentium 4 Prescott was consuming 115 watts and running at temperatures that required increasingly absurd cooling solutions. Intel was projecting that their next-generation chips would need nuclear-reactor-level cooling.

This is the **power wall**: we cannot keep increasing clock speed because chips would melt. The graph below illustrates the trend:

| Year | Processor | Clock Speed | TDP (Watts) |
|------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| 1993 | Pentium | 66 MHz | 15 W |
| 1999 | Pentium III | 500 MHz | 25 W |
| 2004 | Pentium 4 | 3.8 GHz | 115 W |
| 2006 | Core 2 Duo | 2.4 GHz | 65 W |
| 2023 | Core i9-14900K | 6.0 GHz | 253 W |

Notice what happened around 2006: Intel **lowered** clock speeds and added a second core instead. The free lunch was over.

### The Multi-Core Era

Since we cannot make individual cores much faster, chip designers pivoted to putting **more cores** on a single die. Modern CPUs have 8-24 cores. But here is the problem: most software is written **sequentially**. A program that runs on one core cannot magically use 8 cores. Developers must explicitly restructure their code for parallelism.

This is where the story gets interesting. CPUs added cores cautiously -- 2, 4, 8, 16. GPUs took a radically different approach: they went to **thousands** of simpler cores.

### Throughput vs. Latency

To understand why GPUs exist, you need to understand two fundamentally different computing philosophies:

**Latency-oriented design (CPUs):** Minimize the time to complete **one** task. CPUs have massive caches (often 32+ MB of L3 cache), sophisticated branch predictors, out-of-order execution engines, and speculative execution. All of this hardware exists to make a single thread of execution as fast as possible.

> **Analogy:** A CPU is like a Ferrari. It gets **one person** to their destination incredibly fast. It has a powerful engine, excellent handling, and advanced technology. But it can only carry one or two people at a time.

**Throughput-oriented design (GPUs):** Maximize the total work completed per unit time across **all** tasks, even if each individual task is slower. GPUs have tiny caches, no branch prediction, simple in-order cores -- but thousands of them.

> **Analogy:** A GPU is like a city bus system. Each individual bus is slower than a Ferrari. But 100 buses running simultaneously move 5,000 people per hour, while 100 Ferraris move only 200.

The key insight is: **many real-world problems are embarrassingly parallel.** Processing 10 million pixels in an image, multiplying two 4096x4096 matrices, computing gradients for a neural network with 175 billion parameters -- these problems decompose naturally into millions of independent sub-tasks.

### Performance Numbers That Matter

To appreciate the scale difference, consider these peak theoretical throughputs:

| Metric | Intel i9-14900K (CPU) | NVIDIA A100 (GPU) | Ratio |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------|-------|
| FP32 TFLOPS | ~1.0 | 19.5 | 20x |
| FP16 TFLOPS | ~2.0 | 312 | 156x |
| Memory BW (GB/s) | 89.6 | 2,039 | 23x |
| Cores | 24 | 6,912 | 288x |
| TDP (Watts) | 253 | 300 | 1.2x |

The GPU achieves **156x more half-precision FLOPS** while consuming only 20% more power. This is not magic -- it is a fundamentally different design tradeoff. The GPU sacrifices single-thread performance to maximize aggregate throughput.

### Why This Matters for You

If you are training deep learning models, doing scientific simulations, processing large datasets, or working on computer graphics, the GPU is not just "faster" -- it is a **different category** of computing device. Learning to program GPUs is not about learning a new language; it is about learning to think in parallel.

The rest of this course will teach you that parallel thinking, using CUDA through Python's `numba.cuda` library. By the end, you will be writing kernels that run thousands of threads simultaneously, achieving speedups of 10-100x over CPU code.

```python
# A taste of what is coming: CPU vs GPU vector addition
import numpy as np
import time

# CPU: sequential processing
def cpu_vector_add(a, b, c):
    for i in range(len(a)):
        c[i] = a[i] + b[i]

n = 10_000_000
a = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
b = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
c = np.zeros(n, dtype=np.float32)

start = time.perf_counter()
cpu_vector_add(a, b, c)
cpu_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"CPU (pure Python loop): {cpu_time*1000:.1f} ms")

# NumPy (vectorized, uses SIMD on CPU)
start = time.perf_counter()
c = a + b
np_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"NumPy (vectorized CPU): {np_time*1000:.1f} ms")
print(f"Speedup from vectorization: {cpu_time/np_time:.0f}x")
print("Imagine another 10-100x on top of that -- that is GPU computing.")
```

This module will take you from understanding **why** GPUs exist to writing your first GPU kernel. Let us start by looking at what makes GPU architecture so different.
sections.code:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time

# ============================================
# Demonstrating why parallel computing matters
# ============================================

# Task: Add two large vectors element-by-element
# This is "embarrassingly parallel" -- each element is independent

n = 10_000_000
a = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
b = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)

# Method 1: Pure Python loop (sequential, one element at a time)
def python_add(a, b):
    c = np.empty_like(a)
    for i in range(len(a)):
        c[i] = a[i] + b[i]
    return c

# Time the pure Python approach (only first 100K to avoid waiting)
small_a, small_b = a[:100_000], b[:100_000]
start = time.perf_counter()
c_py = python_add(small_a, small_b)
python_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"Pure Python (100K elements): {python_time*1000:.1f} ms")
print(f"  Estimated for 10M elements: {python_time*100*1000:.0f} ms")

# Method 2: NumPy vectorized (uses SIMD instructions on CPU)
start = time.perf_counter()
c_np = a + b
numpy_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"\nNumPy vectorized (10M elements): {numpy_time*1000:.2f} ms")
print(f"  Speedup vs Python: {(python_time*100)/numpy_time:.0f}x")

# Method 3: GPU with numba.cuda
from numba import cuda

@cuda.jit
def gpu_add(a, b, c):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)  # Get global thread index
    if idx < a.size:     # Boundary check
        c[idx] = a[idx] + b[idx]

# Transfer data to GPU
d_a = cuda.to_device(a)
d_b = cuda.to_device(b)
d_c = cuda.device_array_like(a)

# Configure grid: 256 threads per block
threads_per_block = 256
blocks_per_grid = (n + threads_per_block - 1) // threads_per_block

# Warm up
gpu_add[blocks_per_grid, threads_per_block](d_a, d_b, d_c)
cuda.synchronize()

# Timed run
start = time.perf_counter()
gpu_add[blocks_per_grid, threads_per_block](d_a, d_b, d_c)
cuda.synchronize()
gpu_time = time.perf_counter() - start

c_gpu = d_c.copy_to_host()
print(f"\nGPU numba.cuda (10M elements): {gpu_time*1000:.2f} ms")
print(f"  Speedup vs NumPy: {numpy_time/gpu_time:.1f}x")
print(f"  Speedup vs Python: {(python_time*100)/gpu_time:.0f}x")

# Verify correctness
assert np.allclose(c_np, c_gpu, atol=1e-5), "Results don't match!"
print("\nResults verified: GPU output matches CPU output.")

references.title: PMPP Chapter 1: Introduction (Hwu, Kirk, El Hajj)
The Free Lunch Is Over (Herb Sutter, 2005)

sections.title: CPU vs GPU Architecture
sections.content:
## Two Fundamentally Different Machines

A CPU and a GPU are both silicon chips that execute instructions. But they are designed for completely different workloads, and understanding their architectural differences is essential to writing efficient GPU code.

### CPU Architecture: The Swiss Army Knife

A modern CPU core is an engineering marvel optimized for **sequential speed**. Let us break down where the transistors go:

**Control Logic (~50% of die area):** The CPU dedicates enormous resources to making each individual instruction execute as fast as possible:
- **Branch Predictor:** Guesses which way an `if/else` will go before the condition is evaluated. Modern CPUs achieve 95%+ prediction accuracy. When wrong, the pipeline must flush (15-20 cycle penalty).
- **Out-of-Order Execution Engine:** Reorders instructions on-the-fly to avoid stalls. If instruction B depends on A's result but instruction C does not, the CPU runs C while waiting for A.
- **Speculative Execution:** Executes instructions ahead of time, discarding results if the speculation was wrong.
- **Register Renaming:** Maps logical registers to a larger set of physical registers to eliminate false data dependencies.

**Cache Hierarchy (~30% of die area):** CPUs have large, multi-level caches to hide memory latency:

| Cache Level | Size (typical) | Latency | Purpose |
|-------------|---------------|---------|----------|
| L1 Data | 48 KB/core | ~4 cycles | Hot loop variables |
| L1 Instruction | 32 KB/core | ~4 cycles | Current code |
| L2 | 1.25 MB/core | ~12 cycles | Working set |
| L3 (shared) | 32-36 MB | ~40 cycles | Shared data |
| Main Memory | 16-128 GB | ~200 cycles | Everything else |

**ALUs (~20% of die area):** The actual computation units are a surprisingly small fraction of the chip. A modern CPU core might have 4-8 ALUs (Arithmetic Logic Units).

> **Key insight:** Most of the CPU's transistors are spent on making **one thread** run fast, not on doing more computations in parallel.

### GPU Architecture: The Massively Parallel Engine

A GPU flips the CPU's priorities upside down. Instead of making one thread fast, it makes **thousands of threads efficient**.

**ALUs (~80% of die area):** The vast majority of the GPU die is dedicated to computation units. An NVIDIA A100 has 6,912 CUDA cores (FP32 ALUs) and 432 Tensor Cores.

**Minimal Control Logic (~5%):** GPU cores have no branch prediction, no out-of-order execution, no speculative execution. Each core is simple: fetch instruction, decode, execute. Period.

**Small Caches (~15%):** GPUs have tiny caches per core. An A100 has only 192 KB of L1/shared memory per Streaming Multiprocessor (SM), compared to ~1.3 MB of L1+L2 per CPU core.

### How GPUs Hide Latency: The Magic Trick

If GPU cores are so simple and have tiny caches, how do they achieve such high throughput? The answer is **massive thread-level parallelism** as a latency-hiding mechanism.

When a CPU thread hits a cache miss (waiting ~200 cycles for memory), the core stalls -- it has nothing else to do. The out-of-order engine tries to find other instructions to run, but there are limits.

When a GPU thread hits a memory access, the hardware instantly **switches to another thread** at zero cost. There is no context switch overhead because the GPU keeps the register state of thousands of threads resident simultaneously. While threads 0-31 wait for memory, threads 32-63 execute. When those stall, threads 64-95 take over. And so on.

> **Analogy:** Imagine a restaurant kitchen. A CPU chef starts a dish, and when it needs to simmer for 10 minutes, the chef stands there watching the pot. A GPU chef starts 100 dishes simultaneously -- when dish 1 simmers, the chef works on dish 2, then dish 3. The chef is never idle because there is always another dish ready for attention.

This is why GPUs need **thousands of active threads** to achieve peak performance. If you launch only 32 threads on a GPU, it will be slower than a CPU because those 32 simple cores are each slower than a CPU core, and there are not enough threads to hide memory latency.

### SIMT: Single Instruction, Multiple Threads

GPU threads do not execute independently like CPU threads. They execute in groups of 32 called **warps** (NVIDIA terminology). All 32 threads in a warp execute the **same instruction** at the **same time**, but on different data.

This is similar to SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) on CPUs, but with an important difference: SIMT threads have their own program counter and can **diverge**. If a warp hits an `if/else`:

```python
if condition:    # Some threads: True, others: False
    do_A()       # Only threads where condition=True execute
else:
    do_B()       # Only threads where condition=False execute
# All threads reconverge here
```

Both branches execute **sequentially**: first the `if` branch for qualifying threads (others are masked/idle), then the `else` branch. This is called **warp divergence** and it halves your throughput.

> **Rule of thumb:** Avoid `if/else` statements where different threads in the same warp take different paths. When divergence is unavoidable, minimize the work in the divergent branches.

### Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs)

GPU cores are organized into groups called **Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs)**. Each SM contains:

- 64-128 CUDA cores (depending on architecture)
- Shared memory (configurable, typically 48-164 KB)
- L1 cache
- Warp schedulers (2-4 per SM)
- Register file (65,536 32-bit registers on many architectures)

The NVIDIA T4 GPU (common on Google Colab) has:
- **40 SMs**
- **64 FP32 cores per SM** = 2,560 total CUDA cores
- **320 Tensor Cores** (for matrix operations)
- **16 GB GDDR6 memory** at 320 GB/s bandwidth
- **65,536 registers per SM**

Threads are assigned to SMs in units called **thread blocks** (or simply "blocks"). All threads in a block run on the same SM and can share data through shared memory. Threads in different blocks cannot directly communicate (except through global memory).

### Architecture Comparison Summary

```
CPU Core:                          GPU SM:
+---------------------------+      +---------------------------+
| Branch Predictor          |      | Warp Scheduler  | Warp   |
| Out-of-Order Engine       |      | Scheduler       |        |
| Speculation Logic         |      +---------------------------+
+---------------------------+      | Core Core Core Core Core |
|    ALU    |    ALU        |      | Core Core Core Core Core |
+---------------------------+      | Core Core Core Core Core |
| L1 Cache (48 KB)          |      | Core Core Core Core Core |
| L2 Cache (1.25 MB)        |      | Core Core Core Core Core |
+---------------------------+      | Core Core Core Core Core |
                                   | ... (64+ cores total)    |
4-8 ALUs, huge control logic       +---------------------------+
                                   | Shared Memory (48-164KB) |
                                   | L1 Cache (small)         |
                                   +---------------------------+
                                   64+ ALUs, minimal control
```

### What This Means for You as a Programmer

1. **Launch enough threads.** A GPU with 2,560 cores needs tens of thousands of threads to stay busy. Launching 100 threads wastes 96% of the hardware.
2. **Minimize branching.** Warp divergence serializes execution. Structure your code so adjacent threads follow the same path.
3. **Think about data locality.** Memory accesses are expensive. Use shared memory for data reused across threads in a block.
4. **Prefer simple, uniform computation.** GPU cores are simple. Complex control flow with many branches destroys performance.
sections.code:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda

# ============================================
# Exploring GPU hardware properties
# ============================================

# Query GPU device information
device = cuda.get_current_device()
print("=== GPU Device Information ===")
print(f"Device name: {device.name}")
print(f"Compute capability: {device.compute_capability}")
print(f"Max threads per block: {device.MAX_THREADS_PER_BLOCK}")
print(f"Max block dimensions: {device.MAX_BLOCK_DIM_X} x {device.MAX_BLOCK_DIM_Y} x {device.MAX_BLOCK_DIM_Z}")
print(f"Max grid dimensions: {device.MAX_GRID_DIM_X} x {device.MAX_GRID_DIM_Y} x {device.MAX_GRID_DIM_Z}")
print(f"Warp size: {device.WARP_SIZE}")
print(f"Max shared memory per block: {device.MAX_SHARED_MEMORY_PER_BLOCK} bytes")
print(f"Multiprocessor count: {device.MULTIPROCESSOR_COUNT}")

# ============================================
# Demonstrating SIMT and warp divergence
# ============================================

# Kernel WITHOUT divergence: all threads take the same path
@cuda.jit
def no_divergence_kernel(data, out):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < data.size:
        # All threads do the same work
        out[idx] = data[idx] * 2.0 + 1.0

# Kernel WITH divergence: odd/even threads take different paths
@cuda.jit
def divergent_kernel(data, out):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < data.size:
        if idx % 2 == 0:  # Half the warp goes one way
            out[idx] = data[idx] * 2.0 + 1.0
        else:              # Other half goes the other way
            out[idx] = data[idx] * 3.0 - 1.0

n = 10_000_000
data = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
d_data = cuda.to_device(data)
d_out = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)

threads = 256
blocks = (n + threads - 1) // threads

# Warm up
no_divergence_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()
divergent_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()

# Benchmark: No divergence
iterations = 100
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iterations):
    no_divergence_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()
no_div_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iterations

# Benchmark: With divergence
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iterations):
    divergent_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()
div_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iterations

print(f"\n=== Warp Divergence Impact ===")
print(f"No divergence: {no_div_time*1000:.3f} ms")
print(f"With divergence: {div_time*1000:.3f} ms")
print(f"Slowdown from divergence: {div_time/no_div_time:.2f}x")
print("\nNote: Even simple odd/even branching causes measurable slowdown.")
print("In real kernels with complex branching, the penalty can be much worse.")

references.title: PMPP Chapter 2: Heterogeneous Data Parallel Computing
CUDA C++ Programming Guide - SIMT Architecture

sections.title: Heterogeneous Computing
sections.content:
## The Host/Device Model

GPU computing is fundamentally **heterogeneous**: your program runs on two different processors simultaneously. The terminology is precise and consistent throughout CUDA programming:

- **Host:** The CPU and its memory (system RAM). Your Python code runs here. Control flow, file I/O, network requests, and orchestration happen on the host.
- **Device:** The GPU and its memory (VRAM/GDDR). Massively parallel computation happens here. The GPU cannot access your filesystem, network, or most OS services.

A typical GPU program follows this pattern:

```
1. Host: Prepare data (load from disk, preprocess)
2. Host -> Device: Transfer input data to GPU memory
3. Device: Launch kernel (GPU executes thousands of threads)
4. Device -> Host: Transfer results back to CPU memory
5. Host: Use results (save to disk, display, etc.)
```

This is not just a software abstraction -- it reflects the physical hardware. The CPU and GPU are separate chips connected by a bus:

```
+============+          PCIe Bus          +============+
|    CPU     | <========================> |    GPU     |
| (Host)     |    ~16-32 GB/s (PCIe 4)    | (Device)   |
|            |    ~64 GB/s (PCIe 5)       |            |
| System RAM |                            | GPU VRAM   |
| 16-512 GB  |                            | 8-80 GB    |
| ~50 GB/s   |                            | ~2000 GB/s |
+============+                            +============+
```

### The PCIe Bottleneck

Notice the bandwidth numbers. GPU VRAM bandwidth is ~2000 GB/s (A100), but the PCIe bus connecting CPU and GPU is only ~32 GB/s (PCIe 4.0 x16). That is a **60x bottleneck**.

This means that if you constantly transfer data back and forth between CPU and GPU, the transfer time will dominate your computation time. The PCIe bus becomes the straw through which you are trying to drink a firehose.

> **Analogy:** Imagine a factory (GPU) that can process 2,000 packages per second, but the delivery truck (PCIe) can only bring in 32 packages per second. The factory workers are idle 98% of the time waiting for deliveries. The solution? Send large batches infrequently rather than small batches constantly.

### When to Use a GPU

GPUs are not universally faster than CPUs. They excel at specific types of problems:

**Good GPU workloads (high parallelism, regular computation):**
- Matrix multiplication and linear algebra
- Image/video processing (each pixel processed independently)
- Neural network training and inference
- Monte Carlo simulations
- Molecular dynamics
- Signal processing (FFTs)

**Poor GPU workloads (sequential, irregular, or small):**
- File I/O and string processing
- Complex branching logic (compilers, interpreters)
- Small problems (overhead exceeds computation)
- Recursive algorithms with unpredictable depth
- Problems with heavy inter-thread communication

The rule of thumb: **if you can describe the problem as "do the same thing to millions of items independently," a GPU will help.** If the problem is "make a sequence of decisions where each depends on the last," stick with the CPU.

### Amdahl's Law: The Limits of Parallelism

In 1967, Gene Amdahl formulated a law that governs every parallel computing effort. It states that the maximum speedup from parallelizing a program is limited by the portion that must remain sequential:

```
Speedup = 1 / (S + P/N)

where:
  S = fraction of the program that is sequential (cannot be parallelized)
  P = fraction that is parallelizable (P = 1 - S)
  N = number of parallel processors
```

Let us work through concrete examples:

**Example 1: 90% parallelizable (S=0.1, P=0.9)**

| N (processors) | Speedup | Efficiency |
|----------------|---------|------------|
| 1 | 1.0x | 100% |
| 10 | 5.3x | 53% |
| 100 | 9.2x | 9.2% |
| 1,000 | 9.9x | 0.99% |
| 10,000 | 9.99x | 0.1% |
| Infinity | **10x** | ~0% |

Even with infinite processors, 10% sequential code limits you to **10x speedup**. Adding more cores beyond a few hundred is completely wasted.

**Example 2: 99% parallelizable (S=0.01, P=0.99)**

| N (processors) | Speedup |
|----------------|---------|
| 100 | 50x |
| 1,000 | 91x |
| 10,000 | 99x |
| Infinity | **100x** |

With 99% parallelizable code, you can achieve up to 100x speedup. This is much better but still bounded.

**Example 3: Data transfer overhead**

Amdahl's Law applies to more than just computation. Consider a program where:
- Data preparation (sequential): 5% of total time
- Data transfer CPU->GPU: 10% of total time
- GPU computation (parallelizable): 75% of total time
- Data transfer GPU->CPU: 10% of total time

The sequential fraction is S = 0.05 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 0.25 (25%). Maximum speedup with infinite GPU cores: **4x**. The data transfer is killing your performance.

> **Note:** In practice, CUDA streams allow you to **overlap** data transfer with GPU computation, partially mitigating this bottleneck. But the principle remains: minimizing the sequential fraction is critical to scaling performance.

### Strategies to Maximize GPU Utilization

Given Amdahl's Law and the PCIe bottleneck, here are strategies that professional GPU programmers use:

**1. Minimize transfers:** Keep data on the GPU as long as possible. If you have a pipeline of operations (load -> process -> transform -> output), do all processing on the GPU before transferring results back.

```python
from numba import cuda
import numpy as np

# BAD: Transfer back and forth for each operation
d_data = cuda.to_device(data)
kernel_1[blocks, threads](d_data)       # GPU
intermediate = d_data.copy_to_host()     # GPU -> CPU (slow!)
d_data = cuda.to_device(intermediate)    # CPU -> GPU (slow!)
kernel_2[blocks, threads](d_data)        # GPU
result = d_data.copy_to_host()           # GPU -> CPU

# GOOD: Stay on GPU between kernels
d_data = cuda.to_device(data)            # CPU -> GPU (once)
kernel_1[blocks, threads](d_data)        # GPU
kernel_2[blocks, threads](d_data)        # GPU (data stays on device)
result = d_data.copy_to_host()           # GPU -> CPU (once)
```

**2. Overlap computation with transfer:** Modern GPUs support concurrent data transfer and computation using CUDA streams. While the GPU processes batch N, you can transfer batch N+1.

**3. Increase the parallel fraction:** The biggest gains come not from faster GPUs but from parallelizing more of your code. Converting even 5% more of your sequential code to parallel can have a dramatic effect at scale.

**4. Use batching:** Instead of processing one image at a time (small problem, high overhead), process 64 images at once (large problem, amortized overhead). This is why deep learning uses mini-batches.

### The Cost-Benefit Analysis

Before reaching for a GPU, ask yourself:

1. **Is the computation large enough?** Launching a kernel has overhead (~10-50 microseconds). If your computation takes less than 1 millisecond on CPU, the GPU overhead may exceed the speedup.

2. **Is the problem parallelizable?** Can you decompose it into thousands of independent sub-tasks?

3. **Can you minimize transfers?** If you must transfer data every iteration, the PCIe bottleneck may negate the GPU speedup.

4. **Is the arithmetic intensity high enough?** Arithmetic intensity = FLOPs / bytes transferred from memory. Low arithmetic intensity means you are memory-bound, and the GPU's compute advantage is wasted.

| Arithmetic Intensity | Classification | Example | GPU Benefit |
|---------------------|----------------|---------|-------------|
| < 1 FLOP/byte | Memory-bound | Vector addition | Moderate |
| 1-10 FLOP/byte | Mixed | Convolution | Good |
| > 10 FLOP/byte | Compute-bound | Matrix multiply | Excellent |

Matrix multiplication has arithmetic intensity of O(N) -- as matrices grow, each byte of data is used for more and more computations. This is why GPUs dominate linear algebra and deep learning.
sections.code:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda

# ============================================
# Heterogeneous Computing: Host/Device Model
# ============================================

# Step 1: Prepare data on HOST (CPU)
print("=== Host/Device Data Transfer Demo ===")
n = 5_000_000
host_a = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
host_b = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
print(f"Created {n:,} element arrays on CPU")
print(f"Data size: {host_a.nbytes / 1e6:.1f} MB per array")

# Step 2: Transfer to DEVICE (GPU) -- measure the cost
start = time.perf_counter()
d_a = cuda.to_device(host_a)
d_b = cuda.to_device(host_b)
d_c = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)
cuda.synchronize()
transfer_to_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"\nCPU -> GPU transfer: {transfer_to_time*1000:.2f} ms")
print(f"  Effective bandwidth: {3 * host_a.nbytes / transfer_to_time / 1e9:.1f} GB/s")

# Step 3: Compute on DEVICE
@cuda.jit
def vector_add(a, b, c):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < a.size:
        c[idx] = a[idx] + b[idx]

threads = 256
blocks = (n + threads - 1) // threads

vector_add[blocks, threads](d_a, d_b, d_c)  # Warm up
cuda.synchronize()

start = time.perf_counter()
vector_add[blocks, threads](d_a, d_b, d_c)
cuda.synchronize()
compute_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"\nGPU compute: {compute_time*1000:.3f} ms")

# Step 4: Transfer results back to HOST
start = time.perf_counter()
host_c = d_c.copy_to_host()
transfer_from_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"GPU -> CPU transfer: {transfer_from_time*1000:.2f} ms")

# Analysis: Where does the time go?
total = transfer_to_time + compute_time + transfer_from_time
print(f"\n=== Time Breakdown ===")
print(f"CPU -> GPU transfer: {transfer_to_time/total*100:.1f}%")
print(f"GPU computation:     {compute_time/total*100:.1f}%")
print(f"GPU -> CPU transfer: {transfer_from_time/total*100:.1f}%")
print(f"Total:               {total*1000:.2f} ms")

# ============================================
# Amdahl's Law Demonstration
# ============================================
print(f"\n=== Amdahl's Law ===")
print(f"Sequential fraction (transfers): {(transfer_to_time + transfer_from_time)/total*100:.1f}%")
print(f"Parallel fraction (compute):     {compute_time/total*100:.1f}%")
print(f"\nFor vector addition, transfer dominates because arithmetic intensity is LOW.")
print(f"(Only 1 FLOP per 12 bytes loaded -- 2 reads + 1 write of 4-byte floats)")

# ============================================
# Demonstrate: keeping data on GPU is key
# ============================================
print(f"\n=== Pipeline: Stay on GPU ===")

@cuda.jit
def scale_kernel(data, scalar):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < data.size:
        data[idx] *= scalar

# BAD: Transfer back after each step
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(10):
    d_data = cuda.to_device(host_a)
    scale_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, 1.01)
    cuda.synchronize()
    host_a_tmp = d_data.copy_to_host()
cuda.synchronize()
bad_time = time.perf_counter() - start

# GOOD: Stay on GPU for all 10 steps
d_data = cuda.to_device(host_a)
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(10):
    scale_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, 1.01)
cuda.synchronize()
result = d_data.copy_to_host()
good_time = time.perf_counter() - start

print(f"BAD  (transfer each step):  {bad_time*1000:.2f} ms")
print(f"GOOD (stay on GPU):         {good_time*1000:.2f} ms")
print(f"Speedup from avoiding transfers: {bad_time/good_time:.1f}x")

references.title: CUDA C++ Programming Guide - Heterogeneous Programming

quiz.question: What is the 'power wall' in CPU design?
options: The maximum amount of RAM a CPU can address
The physical limit on how many transistors can fit on a chip
The cubic relationship between clock frequency and power consumption that prevents further clock speed increases
The thermal limit of silicon at 100 degrees Celsius
quiz.explanation: The power wall refers to the fact that power consumption grows roughly as Voltage^2 * Frequency. Since voltage must increase with frequency, power grows cubically with clock speed, making further frequency increases impractical due to heat dissipation limits.

quiz.question: Why do GPUs dedicate most of their transistor budget to ALUs rather than control logic?
options: GPUs don't need to execute instructions
GPUs hide memory latency by switching between thousands of threads instead of using branch prediction and out-of-order execution
ALUs are cheaper to manufacture than control logic
GPU programs never have conditional branches
quiz.explanation: GPUs use massive thread-level parallelism to hide latency: when one warp stalls on memory, the hardware instantly switches to another warp at zero cost. This eliminates the need for expensive branch predictors and out-of-order execution engines, freeing transistors for more compute units.

quiz.question: What is a 'warp' in NVIDIA GPU architecture?
options: A single GPU core
A group of 32 threads that execute the same instruction simultaneously
A data transfer unit between CPU and GPU
A unit of shared memory
quiz.explanation: A warp is a group of 32 threads that execute in SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads) fashion. All threads in a warp execute the same instruction at the same time, but each operates on different data. This is the fundamental unit of execution on NVIDIA GPUs.

quiz.question: What happens when threads in the same warp take different branches of an if/else statement?
options: The warp splits into two independent warps
Both branches execute sequentially, with non-participating threads masked off (warp divergence)
The GPU throws a runtime error
One branch is chosen randomly and the other is skipped
quiz.explanation: When threads in a warp diverge, both branches execute serially. First the 'if' branch runs (with non-qualifying threads masked), then the 'else' branch. This effectively halves throughput and is called warp divergence. Threads reconverge after the branch.

quiz.question: According to Amdahl's Law, what is the maximum speedup of a program that is 95% parallelizable, even with infinite processors?
options: Unlimited
quiz.explanation: Amdahl's Law: Speedup = 1 / S, where S is the sequential fraction. With S = 0.05 (5% sequential), the maximum speedup is 1/0.05 = 20x. The 5% sequential portion becomes the bottleneck that no amount of parallelism can overcome.

quiz.question: Why is the PCIe bus a critical bottleneck in GPU computing?
options: PCIe is unreliable and loses data during transfer
PCIe bandwidth (~32 GB/s) is 60x slower than GPU memory bandwidth (~2000 GB/s), so frequent CPU-GPU transfers dominate total execution time
PCIe can only transfer integer data, not floating-point
PCIe requires the GPU to stop computing during transfers
quiz.explanation: GPU VRAM bandwidth is ~2000 GB/s but PCIe 4.0 x16 provides only ~32 GB/s -- a 60x gap. If your program frequently transfers data between CPU and GPU, the transfer time can dominate total execution time, negating the GPU's computational advantage.

quiz.question: Which workload type benefits LEAST from GPU acceleration?
options: Matrix multiplication of large matrices
Image filtering applied to every pixel independently
Recursive tree traversal with unpredictable branching
Training a neural network with mini-batches of 64 images
quiz.explanation: Recursive tree traversal involves sequential decisions (each step depends on the previous), irregular memory access patterns, and heavy branching -- all of which are poorly suited for GPUs. GPUs excel at regular, data-parallel workloads like matrix multiply, image processing, and neural network training.

quiz.question: What is 'arithmetic intensity' and why does it matter for GPU performance?
options: The number of GPU cores used; more cores means more intensity
The ratio of floating-point operations to bytes transferred from memory; higher intensity means the GPU can better utilize its compute capabilities
The clock frequency of the GPU; higher frequency means more intense computation
The complexity of the algorithm; more complex algorithms have higher intensity
quiz.explanation: Arithmetic intensity = FLOPs / bytes transferred. Operations with high arithmetic intensity (like matrix multiplication, O(N) FLOPs per byte) can keep the GPU's compute units busy because each byte of data is reused for many calculations. Low intensity operations (like vector addition, 1 FLOP per 12 bytes) are memory-bound and cannot fully utilize the GPU's compute power.

exercise.title: CPU vs GPU Vector Addition Benchmark
exercise.description:
Implement vector addition on both CPU and GPU, measure transfer times, compute times, and total times, then analyze the results using Amdahl's Law.

**Requirements:**
- Create random float32 vectors of sizes: 1000, 100000, 1000000, 10000000
- For each size, measure: (a) CPU computation time (using NumPy), (b) GPU transfer time (to + from), (c) GPU computation time, (d) total GPU time
- Compute the speedup as CPU_time / GPU_total_time for each size
- Also compute the 'ideal' speedup as CPU_time / GPU_compute_time (ignoring transfers)
- Print results in a clear table
- Discuss: at what size does the GPU become worthwhile, considering transfer overhead?

**Hints:**
- Use `cuda.to_device()` and `.copy_to_host()` for transfers
- Use `cuda.synchronize()` before timing measurements
- Warm up the GPU with one untimed kernel launch before benchmarking
- Use `time.perf_counter()` for precise timing
- The crossover point where GPU beats CPU depends on the operation's arithmetic intensity

        c_cpu = a + b
    cpu_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / num_iters

    # GPU benchmark

    blocks = (n + threads - 1) // threads

    # TODO: Measure GPU compute time (kernel execution)
    # TODO: Measure GPU transfer back time (device -> host)
    # TODO: Calculate total GPU time
    # TODO: Verify correctness

    # Return: cpu_time, transfer_to, compute, transfer_from, total_gpu

# TODO: Test sizes [1_000, 100_000, 1_000_000, 10_000_000]

# TODO: Identify the crossover point
exercise.solution:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda

@cuda.jit
def gpu_vector_add(a, b, c):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < a.size:
        c[idx] = a[idx] + b[idx]

def benchmark_size(n, num_iters=5):
    """Benchmark CPU vs GPU vector addition for a given size."""
    a = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
    b = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)

    # CPU benchmark (NumPy)
    c_cpu = a + b  # Warm up
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        c_cpu = a + b
    cpu_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / num_iters

    # GPU setup
    threads = 256
    blocks = (n + threads - 1) // threads

    # Warm up GPU
    d_a = cuda.to_device(a)
    d_b = cuda.to_device(b)
    d_c = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)
    gpu_vector_add[blocks, threads](d_a, d_b, d_c)
    cuda.synchronize()

    # Measure transfer to GPU
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        d_a = cuda.to_device(a)
        d_b = cuda.to_device(b)
        d_c = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)
        cuda.synchronize()
    transfer_to = (time.perf_counter() - start) / num_iters

    # Measure GPU compute
    d_a = cuda.to_device(a)
    d_b = cuda.to_device(b)
    d_c = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)
    cuda.synchronize()
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        gpu_vector_add[blocks, threads](d_a, d_b, d_c)
        cuda.synchronize()
    compute_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / num_iters

    # Measure transfer from GPU
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        c_gpu = d_c.copy_to_host()
    transfer_from = (time.perf_counter() - start) / num_iters

    total_gpu = transfer_to + compute_time + transfer_from

    # Verify correctness
    assert np.allclose(c_cpu, c_gpu, atol=1e-5), "Results mismatch!"

    return cpu_time, transfer_to, compute_time, transfer_from, total_gpu

# Benchmark at different sizes
sizes = [1_000, 100_000, 1_000_000, 10_000_000]
print(f"{'Size':>12} | {'CPU (ms)':>10} | {'GPU Tot (ms)':>13} | {'Compute (ms)':>13} | {'Xfer (ms)':>10} | {'Speedup':>8} | {'Ideal':>8}")
print("-" * 95)

for n in sizes:
    cpu_t, xfer_to, compute, xfer_from, total = benchmark_size(n)
    xfer = xfer_to + xfer_from
    speedup = cpu_t / total if total > 0 else float('inf')
    ideal = cpu_t / compute if compute > 0 else float('inf')
    print(f"{n:>12,} | {cpu_t*1000:>10.3f} | {total*1000:>13.3f} | {compute*1000:>13.3f} | {xfer*1000:>10.3f} | {speedup:>7.2f}x | {ideal:>7.1f}x")

print("\nNote: GPU 'Ideal' ignores transfer overhead (data already on GPU).")
print("For small sizes, transfer overhead dominates and CPU wins.")
print("For large sizes, GPU compute speed wins despite transfer costs.")
print("Vector addition has LOW arithmetic intensity (1 FLOP / 12 bytes),")
print("so the crossover point is relatively large. Matrix multiplication")
print("(HIGH arithmetic intensity) would show GPU advantages at smaller sizes.")
